attributes.stem:
An 82-year-old man presents to the palliative care ward with recurrent glioblastoma multiforme. He has previously undergone intracranial surgery and radiotherapy. He has a medical history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes. His current medications include levetiracetam, dexamethasone, and metformin. He does not take any regular pain medications.

On examination, he appears drowsy but rousable. His pulse rate is 78/min, blood pressure 132/84 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. His Glasgow Coma Scale score is 14 (E4V4M6).

His family reports that he has been experiencing daily breakthrough seizures over the past week. They have noticed that he is taking longer to recover after these episodes, and believe after his most recent episode he has not returned to his normal level of alertness.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient with recurrent glioblastoma multiforme is experiencing frequent breakthrough seizures despite being on levetiracetam, at with the ongoing reduced level of consciousness raises a possibility of non convulsive status epilepticus. The most appropriate next step in management is to start subcutaneous midazolam.

Benzodiazepines such as midazolam is the preferred first-line treatment for breakthrough seizures in palliative care settings due to its rapid onset of action, short duration, and ease of administration via subcutaneous route. Benzodiazepines such as lorazepam, clonazepam and midazolam can be used for breakthrough seizures and can be given via many routes of administration.

Increasing the oral levetiracetam dose might be considered, but it may not provide immediate relief for the breakthrough seizures. Moreover, the patient is already experiencing prolonged post-ictal states, which could worsen with higher doses of anti-epileptic medications, and practically they may not be able to swallow oral medications with their increased sedation.

Commencing intravenous phenytoin requires insertion of an intravenous cannula and careful monitoring and may have significant drug adverse effects, making it less suitable in this palliative care setting. It is however a potential option, alongside other anticonvulsant medication in intravenous formulations such as levetiracetam, valproate or lacosamide.

Initiating oral clobazam could be an option for long-term seizure management, but it would not address the immediate need for breakthrough seizure control. Additionally, it may contribute to further drowsiness in this already drowsy patient, and practically they may not be able to swallow oral medications with their increased sedation.

Arranging an urgent MRI brain scan is unlikely to change the management at this stage, given the known recurrence of glioblastoma and the palliative care setting. The focus should be on symptom management rather than further investigations.

In palliative care, the goal of seizure management is to balance seizure control with quality of life. Subcutaneous midazolam can be administered easily by nursing staff or family members, providing rapid seizure control without the need for intravenous access. It can be given as needed or as a continuous infusion if seizures are very frequent. After initiating midazolam, the patient's anti-epileptic regimen should be reviewed and adjusted as necessary, potentially considering the addition of other medications or non-pharmacological approaches to optimize seizure control and minimize side effects.
learningPoints: Midazolam is the preferred first-line treatment for breakthrough seizures in palliative care due to its rapid onset and ease of administration.
Regular review and adjustment of anti-epileptic medications is crucial in managing seizures in patients with advanced brain tumours.
The goal of seizure management in palliative care is to balance seizure control with quality of life and minimise side effects.
references: Grönheit W, Popkirov S, Wehner T, Schlegel U, Wellmer J. Practical Management of Epileptic Seizures and Status Epilepticus in Adult Palliative Care Patients. Front Neurol. 2018;9:595. Published 2018 Aug 2. doi:10.3389/fneur.2018.00595. Available online at ; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6082965/

Kaneoka A, Fujimoto SH, Tamura K, Inaji M, Maehara T. Nonconvulsive status epilepticus characteristics in glioma patients: a retrospective study. Discover Oncology. 2023 Mar 7;14(1):30. Available online at ; https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12672-023-00632-3
